front: What is a random sample and why is it a fundamental concept in statistical machine learning?
back: A random sample is a subset of individuals chosen from a larger population, where each individual is selected purely by chance and every member of the population has an equal probability of being chosen. It is fundamental because it ensures the sample is representative of the population, allowing models to learn generalizable patterns and make unbiased inferences about the whole population.

front: Define sampling bias and provide a common example.
back: Sampling bias is a systematic error that occurs when some members of the intended population are systematically more likely to be selected in a sample than others. A common example is selection bias, such as conducting an online survey that only captures responses from people with internet access, underrepresenting those without it.

front: What is the key difference between a population distribution and a sampling distribution?
back: The population distribution describes the values of a variable for the entire group of interest. A sampling distribution describes the probability distribution of a sample statistic (like the sample mean) over many repeated samples drawn from the same population. It shows how the statistic would vary from sample to sample.

front: What does the Central Limit Theorem (CLT) state about the sampling distribution of the mean?
back: The Central Limit Theorem states that for a sufficiently large sample size (n), the sampling distribution of the sample mean will be approximately normally distributed, regardless of the shape of the population distribution. The mean of this sampling distribution equals the population mean (μ), and its standard deviation (the standard error) is σ/√n.

front: How can non-random sampling (like convenience sampling) negatively impact a machine learning model?
back: Non-random sampling can introduce bias, meaning the training data is not representative of the real-world data the model will encounter. This leads to a model that learns patterns specific to the biased sample, resulting in poor performance, inaccurate predictions, and flawed conclusions when deployed on new, unbiased data.
